questions.text: What is the size of parvovirus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 200nm is too large for a parvovirus. Parvoviruses are actually among the smallest DNA viruses.
This option is incorrect. Parvoviruses are the smallest known animal viruses, measuring only 18–26 nanometers in diameter.
Parvoviruses are non-enveloped, icosahedral particles 18 to 26 nm in diameter. Plus and minus DNA strands are packaged into separate virions in approximately equal proportions.
This option is incorrect and is not the size of parvovirus. Parvoviruses are the smallest known animal viruses, measuring only 18–26 nanometers in diameter.

questions.explanation: Parvoviruses are the smallest known animal viruses, measuring only 18–26 nanometers in diameter. They are non-enveloped viruses with an icosahedral capsid and contain a single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) genome. Because of their tiny size and simple structure, parvoviruses rely heavily on the host cell’s machinery for replication. An example is the human parvovirus B19, which causes fifth disease in children.

questions.text: HIV is a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV is a virus. Viruses are small, infectious agents that can only replicate inside living cells. They are made up of genetic material (DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat. HIV is a retrovirus, which means that its genetic material is RNA. HIV is able to infect and damage the cells of the immune system, which makes it difficult for the body to fight off infections.

Prions are infectious proteins that can cause diseases such as Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. Prions are misfolded proteins that can cause other proteins to misfold. This can lead to the destruction of brain cells and the development of neurological symptoms.

Viroids are even smaller than viruses and do not have a protein coat. They are made up of only RNA and can cause diseases such as potato spindle tuber viroid. Viroids are able to infect plants and cause a variety of symptoms, including stunted growth, leaf curling, and wilting.

Venom is a poisonous substance that is produced by animals such as snakes, spiders, and scorpions. Venom is injected into the victim's body through the animal's fangs or stinger. Venom can cause a variety of symptoms, depending on the animal that produced it. Some venoms can be fatal, while others only cause mild discomfort.

questions.text: Which was the first virus to be successfully crystallized?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: TMV (tobacco mosaic virus) is the first virus to be successfully crystallized.The filterable agents were first purified in 1935, when Stanley was successful in crystallizing the tobacco mosaicvirus.

TMV was the first virus ever crystallized. In 1935, Stanley managed to purify these filterable infectious agents and obtained crystals of the tobacco mosaic virus, marking a major milestone in virology.

TMV became the first virus to be crystallized when Stanley, in 1935, purified these filterable agents and produced crystals of the tobacco mosaic virus. This discovery helped confirm that viruses could be studied like chemical substances.

In 1935, Stanley purified the tobacco mosaic virus and crystallized it, making TMV the first virus to be successfully crystallized. This work showed that viruses could exist in a form similar to simple chemical compounds.

questions.text: The viruses are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are not considered cellular organisms because they lack structures essential to cells, such as a cell membrane and organelles. They cannot independently carry out life processes like metabolism or reproduction.

Prokaryotes, such as bacteria, are unicellular organisms with a simpler structure than eukaryotes but still have cell membranes and cytoplasm. Viruses lack these features and are not classified as prokaryotes.

Viruses are non-cellular entities made up of genetic material encased in a protein coat. They do not possess cellular structures or the ability to perform cellular functions independently, making them distinct from all cellular life forms.
Eukaryotes have complex cells with a nucleus and organelles. Viruses lack these cellular components and cannot perform the functions of eukaryotic cells, such as energy production and reproduction.

Viruses are extremely small, typically ranging from 20 to 300 nanometers, which is far too small to be seen without a microscope. Observing viruses requires advanced techniques like electron microscopy.

questions.explanation: Viruses are classified as non-cellular because they lack the cellular structures that define living cells, such as a cell membrane, cytoplasm, and organelles. Instead, they consist of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat called a capsid. Unlike cellular organisms, viruses cannot perform metabolic processes or reproduce on their own; they must infect a host cell to replicate. This distinguishes them from cellular life forms, including prokaryotes and eukaryotes.Option A (Cellular) is incorrect because viruses do not have the structures that define cellular life. Option B (Prokaryotes) is incorrect because viruses do not fit the definition of prokaryotic cells, which have a cell membrane and cytoplasm. Option D (Eukaryotes) is incorrect because viruses do not have the complex structures found in eukaryotic cells. Option E (Visible with naked eye) is incorrect because viruses are too small to be seen without specialized equipment.

questions.text: Which of the following is the simplest form of pathogens causing diseases?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses, while simple compared to many other life forms, still consist of genetic material (DNA or RNA) encapsulated in a protein coat. They require a host cell to reproduce and cannot independently cause disease, making them more complex than prions.

Prions are infectious agents that consist solely of misfolded proteins without any nucleic acid. They are considered the simplest form of pathogens because they lack cellular structure and replication machinery, which distinguishes them from other pathogens.

Fungi are complex organisms with defined cellular structures and reproductive mechanisms. They require specific environmental conditions to grow and are not the simplest pathogens, as they are multicellular or unicellular with extensive biological functions.

Amoebas are single-celled organisms that can cause diseases, but they are more complex than prions, as they possess cellular structures, organelles, and various pathogenic mechanisms. Thus, they do not represent the simplest form of pathogens.

questions.explanation: Prions are the simplest disease-causing pathogens because they are merely misfolded proteins with with no nucleic acid (no DNA or RNA), making them the most minimal infectious agents compared to viruses, fungi, or protozoa like amoeba.

questions.text: Which of the following diseases is sexually transmitted?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tuberculosis is caused by bacteria that spread from person to person through microscopic droplets released into the air. This can happen when someone with the untreated, active form of tuberculosis coughs, speaks, sneezes, spits, laughs, or sings. Hence, Option A is incorrect.

Most people get the virus by having unprotected sex with someone who has HIV. Another common way of getting it is by sharing drug needles with someone who is infected with HIV. Hence, Option B is correct.

When a mosquito bites a person infected with a dengue virus, the virus enters the mosquito. Then, when the infected mosquito bites another person, the virus enters that person's bloodstream and causes an infection. Hence, Option C is incorrect.

A person can get cholera by drinking water or eating food contaminated with cholera bacteria. In an epidemic, the source of the contamination is usually the feces of an infected person that contaminates water or food. Hence, Option D is incorrect.

questions.text: Which of the following are not polymer?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Proteins are formed by long chains of amino acids, which are their monomers. Hence, they are polymers.
Polysaccharides consist of repeated units of monosaccharides, making them polymers.
Lipids do not have a repetitive monomer structure and are not considered polymers. Their structures are diverse and include components like fatty acids and glycerol.
Nucleic acids are formed from long chains of nucleotides, their monomers, thus classifying them as polymers.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Lipids. Unlike proteins, polysaccharides, and nucleic acids, lipids do not consist of repeating monomer units and therefore are not classified as polymers. Proteins are polymers because they are made up of amino acids linked in chains. Polysaccharides are polymers of monosaccharides, and nucleic acids are polymers of nucleotides, each consisting of a series of repeated structural units.

questions.text: Maximum number of ATP is obtained from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While glucose is a primary energy source, its complete oxidation yields approximately 30-32 ATP molecules, which is less than fatty acid oxidation.

The complete beta-oxidation of palmitic acid (C16) and subsequent oxidative phosphorylation can yield about 106 ATP molecules. Fatty acids generally produce more ATP than carbohydrates due to their higher reduced carbon content.
Malic acid enters the citric acid cycle and can yield approximately 8-10 ATP molecules, which is significantly less than what is produced from the oxidation of glucose or fatty acids.
The ATP yield from β-amino acids varies widely based on the specific amino acid and its catabolic pathway, but they generally yield less ATP compared to carbohydrates and fatty acids.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Palmitic acid. The complete beta-oxidation of palmitic acid and subsequent oxidative phosphorylation yield about 106 ATP molecules, which is significantly higher than the ATP yield from glucose, malic acid, or β-amino acids. This is because fatty acids have more reduced carbon, providing more energy upon oxidation. Glucose, though a primary energy source, yields only about 30-32 ATP molecules. Malic acid yields even less, at about 8-10 ATP molecules, and the ATP yield from β-amino acids is variable but generally lower than that from carbohydrates and fatty acids.

questions.text: The two polypeptides of human insulin are linked together by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The interatomic linkage that results from the sharing of an electron pair between two atoms. The binding arises from the electrostatic attraction of their nuclei for the same electrons.

The disulphide bond stabilizes the folded form of a protein in several ways: It holds two portions of the protein together, biasing the protein towards the folded topology. That is, the disulphide bond destabilizes the unfolded form of the protein by lowering its entropy.

A hydrogen bond is formed by the interaction of a hydrogen atom that is covalently bonded to an electronegative atom (donor) with another electronegative atom (acceptor). Hydrogen bonding confers rigidity to the protein structure and specificity to intermolecular interactions.

A phosphodiester bond is a chemical bond that forms when exactly two hydroxyl groups in phosphoric acid react with a hydroxyl group on other molecules forming ester bonds. It is found in the DNA and RNA backbone.

questions.explanation: Human insulin is made up of 51 amino acids arranged in two polypeptide chains. one alpha and one beta chain. The alpha chain contains 21 amino acids while the beta chain contains 30 amino acids. The two polypeptide chains are interconnected by disulfide bridges or S-­S linkages.

questions.text: Reactions in which simple substances are combined to form complex substances are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Metabolic reactions are catabolic reactions. Catabolic reactions break down larger and complex substances into smaller and simpler substance.
Catabolic reactions break down larger and complex substances into smaller and simpler substance.
In anabolic reactions, reactants are added together and helps in forming complex substances.
None of the above is wrong as option C is correct.

questions.explanation: Anabolic reactions, also known as anabolism, are the metabolic processes in which smaller molecules are combined to form larger and more complex molecules. These reactions require energy input and are involved in building and synthesizing new molecules within the body. Anabolic reactions are responsible for growth, repair, and maintenance of body tissues and structures.

questions.text: In DNA molecules, adenine pairs with which of the following nucleic acid bases?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Guanine pairs with cytosine in both DNA and RNA, not adenine.
Uracil pairs with adenine in RNA, but DNA contains thymine instead of uracil.
Cytosine pairs with guanine in both DNA and RNA, not adenine.
In DNA, adenine pairs specifically with thymine, forming a stable base pair that is crucial for the double helix structure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Thymine. In DNA, adenine (A) forms hydrogen bonds with thymine (T), creating one of the two primary base pairs that stabilize the DNA double helix structure. This pairing is specific to DNA, as opposed to RNA, where adenine pairs with uracil (U). Guanine (G) pairs with cytosine (C) in both DNA and RNA. Therefore, options A, B, and C are incorrect because they do not reflect the correct base pairing with adenine in DNA.

questions.text: Which bond is the potential source of chemical energy for cellular activities?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: C-N bond is a peptide bond which is not a source of energy for all the cellular activities. So, this option is not correct.

The primary function of the carbon-oxygen (C-O) bond in biology is to form the stable structural backbone of the essential organic molecules of life and to provide sites for the formation of crucial polar functional groups that enable diverse chemical reactions and interactions. So, this is not correct.

C-H bond provides energy for all the cellular activites. In biology, the energy associated with C-H bonds serves as the primary form of long-term chemical energy storage in organic molecules such as glucose, fats, and proteins. This stored potential energy is released in a controlled manner through metabolic processes like cellular respiration to power all life functions. So, this option is correct.

In biology, the function of bond energy is to measure the strength of chemical bonds and to determine the energy released or absorbed during chemical reactions. This is crucial for processes like the breakdown of food molecules, the synthesis of new molecules, and the transfer of energy within cells. So, this is not correct.

questions.explanation: Carbon-oxygen association in glycosidic linkages provides stability to the complex carbohydrate molecules, Carbon combines with nitrogen in amino acid linkages to form peptide bonds and forms proteins. Affinity of hydrogen for high electronegative oxygen is responsible for hydrogen bonding.

questions.text: Which level of protein structure maintains the helix shape of enzymes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Primary Structure (Option A): This is the linear sequence of amino acids in a polypeptide chain, linked by peptide bonds. While the primary structure determines the higher-order structures (including the secondary structure), it does not itself maintain the helix shape. It's like the alphabet that forms words, but not the structure of the paragraphs.

The most common types of secondary structures are the α helix and the β pleated sheet. Both structures are held in shape by hydrogen bonds, which form between the carbonyl O of one amino acid and the amino H of another.

Tertiary Structure (Option C): This refers to the overall three-dimensional folding of a single polypeptide chain, including its secondary structures (like helices and sheets) and irregular loops. It's stabilized by interactions between the side chains (R-groups) of amino acids, such as hydrophobic interactions, ionic bonds, hydrogen bonds, and disulfide bridges. While helices are part of the tertiary structure, the tertiary structure describes the global folding rather than maintaining the local helical shape itself.

Quaternary Structure (Option D): This level of structure applies to proteins composed of two or more polypeptide chains (subunits). It describes the arrangement of these multiple subunits in a functional protein complex. The interactions are similar to those in tertiary structure but occur between different polypeptide chains. It is not involved in maintaining the internal helical shape of individual polypeptide chains.
Both A and B (Option E): As explained, primary structure does not maintain the helix shape, only secondary structure does. So, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The most common types of secondary structures are the α helix and the β pleated sheet. Both structures are held in shape by hydrogen bonds, which form between the carbonyl O of one amino acid and the amino H of another.

questions.text: Pick up the one which is not a lipid:

options.explanation: Acylglycerols, also known as triglycerides, and waxes are types of lipids. They both consist of fatty acid chains attached to a glycerol backbone. Acylglycerols are the main components of dietary fats and oils, while waxes are found in various natural sources, including plant cuticles and animal coatings.

Phospholipids and sphingolipids are also types of lipids. Phospholipids are a major component of cell membranes and consist of a hydrophilic head and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails. Sphingolipids are another class of lipids found in cell membranes and have a sphingosine backbone.
Carotenoids and steroids are not directly lipids but are derivative of lipids but they are still included in family of lipids.

These are not lipids. Dextrins and pectins are types of carbohydrates. Dextrins are produced by the partial hydrolysis of starch and are commonly used as food additives. Pectins are complex polysaccharides found in the cell walls of plants and are involved in maintaining cell structure.

questions.text: In living organisms, the lubricant which provides protection against damage resulting from friction is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Water is essential for life and participates in numerous biological processes, including digestion, circulation, and chemical reactions. Beyond these roles, it primarily acts as a lubricant in the body, cushioning joints, facilitating smooth movement of tissues, and reducing friction between organs. In structures such as the eyes, mouth, and joints, water ensures proper function and prevents wear or damage. Its lubricating property is therefore crucial for maintaining tissue integrity and overall physiological efficiency.

Carbohydrates serve primarily as a source of energy for the body, providing fuel for cellular activities and metabolic processes. Unlike water, they do not act as lubricants in biological systems and have little direct role in reducing friction or protecting tissues. Their main function is to supply readily available energy in the form of glucose, glycogen, and other saccharides to support growth, movement, and overall physiological activity.

Lipids do not function as lubricants to prevent friction in biological systems. Their primary roles are energy storage, forming structural components of cell membranes, and serving as signaling molecules. While certain lipid-based substances, like oils, may reduce friction externally, within the body lipids are not involved in cushioning or lubricating tissues like water is.

Proteins perform a wide range of essential functions in the body, including providing structural support, catalyzing biochemical reactions as enzymes, and regulating physiological processes. However, they are not primarily involved in lubrication or reducing friction between tissues. While some specialized proteins may contribute indirectly to tissue function, water remains the main biological lubricant in living organisms.

questions.explanation: Water is essential for life and participates in numerous biological processes, including digestion, circulation, and chemical reactions. Beyond these roles, it primarily acts as a lubricant in the body, cushioning joints, facilitating smooth movement of tissues, and reducing friction between organs. In structures such as the eyes, mouth, and joints, water ensures proper function and prevents wear or damage. Its lubricating property is therefore crucial for maintaining tissue integrity and overall physiological efficiency.

questions.text: The dark reactions of photosynthesis are characterized by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Synthesis of ATP, O2 and NADH
options.explanation: This is incorrect because ATP and NADH are produced in the light reactions, not the dark reactions. Oxygen (O₂) is also released during the light reactions.

options.text: Utilization of ATP, CO2 and NADPH
options.explanation: The dark reactions of photosynthesis, also known as the Calvin Cycle, do not directly require light to occur, but they do depend on the ATP and NADPH produced during the light reactions. The main events during the dark reactions include:Utilization of ATP: ATP provides the energy necessary for the various steps of the Calvin Cycle.Utilization of NADPH: NADPH provides the reducing power (electrons) needed to convert carbon dioxide (CO₂) into glucose.CO₂ is fixed (incorporated) into organic molecules in the cycle, ultimately leading to the formation of glucose.

options.text: Electron transfer from NADPH to RuBP
options.explanation: This is not correct. In the Calvin Cycle, NADPH donates electrons to reduce intermediates, but it does not transfer electrons to RuBP. Instead, RuBP acts as the acceptor for CO₂ during carbon fixation.

options.text: Carbon dioxide transfer from RuBP to glucose
options.explanation: This is also incorrect because CO₂ is fixed into RuBP during the Calvin Cycle, not transferred from RuBP to glucose. The process ultimately leads to the production of glucose, but it is not a direct transfer.

questions.explanation: Dark reaction is also called carbon-fixing reaction. It is a light-independent process in which sugar molecules are formed from carbon dioxide, water molecules, ATP, and NADPH(products from the light-dependent reaction). The dark reaction occurs in the stroma of the chloroplast, where they utilize the products of the light reaction.

questions.text: Formation of _ will be greater with the faster breakdown of glucose and glycogen to compensate for energy requirements in anaerobic respiration.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Enzymes facilitate biochemical reactions, but they are not the end products of glucose or glycogen breakdown in anaerobic respiration.
Hormones regulate physiological processes but are not byproducts of anaerobic glucose or glycogen breakdown.
Lactic acid is a byproduct of anaerobic respiration, which occurs when glucose and glycogen are broken down without oxygen to meet energy demands.
Fat is typically metabolized through aerobic pathways and is not a byproduct of anaerobic respiration.

questions.explanation: In anaerobic respiration, when oxygen is scarce, glucose and glycogen are broken down to meet energy needs, resulting in lactic acid as a byproduct. This process does not produce enzymes or hormones as byproducts. Fat metabolism mainly occurs in the presence of oxygen and is not involved in this type of respiration.

questions.text: Cytochrome c reduces which coenzyme in the ETC?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cytochrome c transfers electrons to cytochrome a, which is a component of Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase) in the electron transport chain. This is the correct downstream acceptor in the chain after cytochrome c.

There is no such component as "cytochrome q" in the ETC. This option may be confused with Coenzyme Q (ubiquinone), which comes before cytochrome c in the ETC, not after. Also, coenzyme Q accepts electrons from Complex I and II, not from cytochrome c.

Cytochrome b is a part of Complex III (cytochrome bc1 complex) and is upstream of cytochrome c. Cytochrome c actually receives electrons from cytochrome b (via cytochrome c1), not the other way around. So, cytochrome c does not reduce cytochrome b.
While cytochrome a3 is part of Complex IV and receives electrons after cytochrome a, cytochrome c first reduces cytochrome a, not a3. So, a3 is not the direct electron acceptor from cytochrome c.

questions.explanation: In the ETC, electrons are passed down a chain of carriers from higher to lower energy states. The Krebs cycle is the second phase of aerobic respiration. Since glycolysis produces two pyruvates and hence two acetyl-CoAs from one molecule of glucose, the net yield of NADH₂ from the Krebs cycle for one glucose is 6. For one pyruvate, one acetyl-CoA condenses to form citrate, which undergoes oxidative decarboxylation, forming 3 molecules of NADH₂ and one molecule of FADH₂.

questions.explanation: From one molecule of pyruvate passing through the Krebs cycle, three molecules of NADH are formed. During the cycle, pyruvate is first converted into acetyl-CoA, which then enters the Krebs cycle. As the acetyl-CoA is oxidized, hydrogen atoms are transferred to NAD⁺, forming NADH at three different steps. These NADH molecules later carry high-energy electrons to the electron transport chain, where they are used to produce ATP. Thus, each pyruvate generates three NADH molecules in the Krebs cycle.

questions.text: A biochemical process which occurs within a cell to breakdown complex compounds to produce energy is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Respiration is a biochemical process that occurs within a cell to break down complex compounds, such as glucose, to produce energy in the form of ATP.

Photosynthesis is the process by which plants and some other organisms convert light energy into chemical energy (glucose) using carbon dioxide and water in the presence of sunlight. It does not involve the breakdown of complex compounds to produce energy.

Oxidation-reduction, also known as redox reactions, involve the transfer of electrons from one molecule to another. While oxidation-reduction reactions play a role in cellular respiration, it is not the specific term for the overall process of breaking down complex compounds to produce energy.
Photophosphorylation is the process of generating ATP using light energy during photosynthesis. It does not involve the breakdown of complex compounds to produce energy.

questions.explanation: The biochemical process that occurs within a cell to break down complex compounds to produce energy is called cellular respiration. Cellular respiration is a set of metabolic reactions that take place in cells to extract energy from organic molecules, such as glucose. The energy released during cellular respiration is used to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which serves as the primary energy currency for cellular activities.

questions.text: ATP formation during photosynthesis is known as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phosphorylation is a biochemical process in which a phosphate group is added to a molecule, typically a protein, lipid, or nucleic acid. This process is crucial for many cellular functions, as it serves as a key regulatory mechanism that can alter the structure, function, and activity of the target molecule. Phosphorylation and dephosphorylation, the removal of a phosphate group, are reversible processes that play essential roles in cellular signalling, metabolism, and various other physiological processes.

Dephosphorylation is the opposite process of phosphorylation, involving the removal of a phosphate group from a molecule, typically a protein, lipid, or nucleic acid. This process is catalysed by enzymes called phosphatases. Dephosphorylation is a crucial regulatory mechanism in cellular signalling and other biological processes, often counteracting the effects of phosphorylation.

Oxidative phosphorylation is a crucial process that occurs in the inner mitochondrial membrane and is responsible for the production of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the primary energy currency of cells. It is the final stage of cellular respiration, following glycolysis and the citric acid cycle (Krebs cycle). Oxidative phosphorylation involves the transfer of electrons through a series of protein complexes in the inner mitochondrial membrane, known as the electron transport chain (ETC), and the coupling of this electron flow to the synthesis of ATP.

Substrate-level phosphorylation is a type of phosphorylation in which a high-energy phosphate group is directly transferred from a phosphorylated substrate molecule to adenosine diphosphate (ADP), forming adenosine triphosphate (ATP). This process occurs during glycolysis and the citric acid cycle (Krebs cycle), which are two stages of cellular respiration. Unlike oxidative phosphorylation, which takes place in the inner mitochondrial membrane, substrate-level phosphorylation occurs in the cytoplasm and mitochondrial matrix.

questions.explanation: Photophosphorylation is the correct term for ATP formation during photosynthesis because it specifically refers to the process in which light energy is used to add a phosphate group to ADP, forming ATP. This process occurs in the chloroplasts and is directly driven by light, which excites electrons in the photosystems and creates a proton gradient to power ATP synthase. Dephosphorylation is the removal of a phosphate group from a molecule, which releases energy rather than storing it as ATP. Oxidative phosphorylation occurs in mitochondria during cellular respiration, where electrons from nutrients pass through an electron transport chain to generate ATP using oxygen, not light. Substrate-level phosphorylation involves the direct transfer of a phosphate group from a high-energy substrate molecule to ADP, typically during glycolysis or the Krebs cycle, and does not require light energy. Therefore, only photophosphorylation correctly describes ATP formation in photosynthesis.

Mitochondria are membrane-bound cell organelles that generate most of the chemical energy needed to power the cell's biochemical reactions. Chemical energy produced by the mitochondria is stored in a small molecule called ATP.

questions.text: Which of the following is left behind when a plant cell is treated by cellulase and pectinase enzymes?

Leucoplasts are colorless organelles found in plant cells that are involved in storing and synthesizing various substances, such as starch, oils, or pigments. questions.text: Which one of the following has its own DNA?

options.explanation: Correct. Mitochondria are unique among organelles as they possess their own circular DNA, which is distinct from the nuclear DNA of the cell. This DNA is involved in coding for essential proteins required for energy production through cellular respiration.

In contrast, dictyosomes, lysosomes, and peroxisomes are all organelles that do not contain their own genetic material. Dictyosomes function in the modification and packaging of proteins, lysosomes are involved in the digestion of cellular waste, and peroxisomes play roles in lipid metabolism and detoxification. questions.text: A loaded and an unloaded cart are moving with same kinetic energies such that same retarding force acts on them and they finally stop after covering “S1” and “S2” distances respectively, which of the following is true?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Since the loaded and unloaded carts are moving with the same kinetic energies and experience the same retarding force, we can apply the work-energy principle to determine the relationship between S1 and S2.The work-energy principle states that "the work done on an object is equal to the change in its kinetic energy." Mathematically, it can be expressed as:Work = ΔKinetic EnergyFor both the loaded and unloaded carts, the initial kinetic energy is the same;since they are moving with the same kinetic energy. When they finally stop, their final kinetic energy becomes zero.For the loaded cart:Work = Change in kinetic energyWork = Final kinetic energy - Initial kinetic energyWork = 0 - Initial kinetic energyFor the unloaded cart:Work = Change in kinetic energyWork = Final kinetic energy - Initial kinetic energyWork = 0 - Initial kinetic energySince the work done on both carts is the same (retarding force is the same), we can equate the work expressions:0 - Initial kinetic energy = 0 - Initial kinetic energyThis simplifies to:0 = 0The equation is always true, indicating that the initial kinetic energies of both the loaded and unloaded carts are equal. Since kinetic energy is proportional to the square of velocity (KE = 0.5 * m * v^2), this means that the squares of their velocities are equal.Let's denote the initial velocities of the loaded and unloaded carts as vloaded and vunloaded, respectively.vloaded^2 = vunloaded^2Now, we can relate the distances S1 and S2 covered by the loaded and unloaded carts, respectively.The work done on an object can also be expressed as the force multiplied by the displacement: Work = Force * Distance.Since the retarding force is the same for both carts, we can equate the work expressions again:Force * S1 = Force * S2Dividing both sides by the force (since it's the same for both):S1 = S2So, the relationship between S1 and S2 is that they are equal. Both carts cover the same distance before finally stopping, even though one is loaded and the other is unloaded.

questions.text: A limiting reactant is the one:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.text: Which is present in maximum amount
options.explanation: This option suggests that the limiting reactant is the one present in the maximum amount. However, this is not true. The limiting reactant is the reactant that is entirely consumed or "limits" the amount of product that can be formed in a chemical reaction. The limiting reactant is not determined by the initial quantities of reactants but rather by their stoichiometric ratios. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Which produces minimum no. of moles of product
options.explanation: This option accurately describes the concept of a limiting reactant. In a chemical reaction, the stoichiometry of the balanced equation determines the theoretical amount of product that can be formed from each reactant. The reactant that produces the minimum number of moles of product is the limiting reactant because it determines the maximum amount of product that can be obtained. Once the limiting reactant is completely consumed, the reaction stops and no further product can be formed. Therefore, this option is correct.

options.text: Which produces maximum no. of moles of product
options.explanation: The reactant that produces the maximum number of moles of product is not the limiting reactant but rather the reactant in excess. The reactant in excess is not entirely consumed and remains present after the reaction is complete. The limiting reactant is the one that determines the extent of the reaction and sets the maximum amount of product that can be obtained. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Does not affect the amount of product
options.explanation: This option is also incorrect. The limiting reactant directly affects the amount of product that can be formed. As mentioned earlier, the limiting reactant determines the maximum amount of product that can be obtained. If one of the reactants is limiting, it will restrict the reaction's progress and limit the amount of product that can be produced. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: To summarize, the correct answer is option B) "Which produces the minimum number of moles of product". It accurately describes the concept of a limiting reactant. In a chemical reaction, the stoichiometry of the balanced equation determines the theoretical amount of product that can be formed from each reactant. The reactant that produces the minimum number of moles of product is the limiting reactant because it determines the maximum amount of product that can be obtained. Once the limiting reactant is completely consumed, the reaction stops and no further product can be formed.

questions.text: To conduct electricity through Ionic Solids, the ions should be:

"Energized" generally means that a system or particle has gained energy. In the case of ionic solids, energy is indeed required to initiate the conduction of electricity, but it is not the ions themselves that need to be energized. Instead, the energy typically comes from an external source like an electric field, which allows the ions to move and conduct electricity.

The correct answer is "c) Free." For a material to conduct electricity, it must have mobile charge carriers. When an ionic solid is dissolved in water or melted, the ions become free and can move, resulting in electrical conductivity.

questions.text: When water is poured into a glass test tube, the surface of the water is concave. This meniscus forms because of:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: If the adhesive forces (the attraction between the liquid and the glass) were weak, the water would not want to touch the walls of the tube. Instead, it would pull away and try to form a ball in the center, which would not result in a concave shape.

This is the correct option. A concave meniscus forms because the adhesive forces between the water molecules and the glass walls are stronger than the cohesive forces between the water molecules themselves. Because water "prefers" to stick to the glass more than to itself, it actually climbs up the sides of the tube, creating the curved downward dip in the middle.

Strong cohesive forces (attraction between like molecules) actually cause a convex meniscus. This is seen in mercury, where the atoms are so strongly attracted to each other that they pull away from the glass, creating a dome-like or upward-curving shape.

If the adhesive forces and cohesive forces were exactly equal, the surface of the liquid would remain perfectly flat or horizontal. There would be no tendency for the liquid to either climb the walls or pull away from them, resulting in no curvature at all.

questions.explanation: The shape of the liquid surface (meniscus) is a battle between two forces: adhesion and cohesion. For water in glass, the glass is very polar, so water molecules "stick" to it very strongly through adhesion. Because this attraction to the glass is stronger than the internal "pull" of water molecules on each other (cohesion), the water edges crawl upward. This result is the bowl-shaped or concave surface that we always measure from the bottom.
